description: Amicus Brief of the Association in Support of Respondent
plain_text: INTEREST OF AMICUS CURIAE. The Association submits this brief to bring to the Court's attention a body of institutional experience that the parties, focused on their own dispute, have not developed. We take no position on which side should prevail on the facts; our concern is with the rule the Court will announce and how it will operate across the many cases the parties cannot see. SUMMARY OF ARGUMENT. The standard the petitioner proposes is administrable in the abstract but unworkable in practice, because it asks trial courts to make a determination they are institutionally poorly placed to make and that will, predictably, be made inconsistently. We urge the Court to adopt the narrower rule the respondent defends, not because it is tidier, but because it allocates the decision to the actor best positioned to make it and leaves the harder questions to be resolved on a developed record rather than at the threshold. ARGUMENT. Three features of the petitioner's rule deserve the Court's attention. First, it requires a predictive judgment at a stage when the facts that would inform the prediction are not yet before the court. Second, it offers no limiting principle, so that what is presented as a narrow exception will, by its own logic, swallow the rule it qualifies. Third, it disregards the reliance interests of the many institutions that have ordered their affairs around the settled understanding the petitioner would unsettle. None of these objections depends on the equities of the present case; each would hold whichever party had advanced the rule. We close by addressing the petitioner's reliance on a line of authority that, on inspection, supports the respondent: in each cited case the Court declined to take the step the petitioner now asks it to take. CONCLUSION. The judgment below should be affirmed.
